(3) It is part of the purchase price when that 
transaction is carried out.
(4) It is forfeited when the transaction falls through 
by reason of the default or failure of the purchaser.
(5) Unless there is anything to the contrary in the 
terms of the contract, on default committed by the 
buyer, the seller is entitled to forfeit the earnest.’”
(Emphasis supplied)
33. In the case of Videocon Properties Ltd. v. Bhalchandra 
Laboratories reported in (2004) 3 SCC 711, while assessing 
the difference between “advance” and “earnest”, this Court took 
the view that the words used in the agreement alone cannot be 
determinative of the true nature of the amount advanced. Instead, 
the intention of the parties and the surrounding circumstances serve 
as more apt indicators. Further, the Court observed that earnest 
money fulfils a dual purpose: first, it operates as part-payment of 
the purchase price and; secondly, as security for the performance
